serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Fish Kebab (Balik Shish) is approximately 300 g, comprising grilled marinated fish cubes on skewers with garlic-cream sauce, a side salad, and a choice of butter rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Kebab (Balik Shish) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fish Kebab (Balik Shish), cr_id G00010179, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on grilled fish with mixed accompaniments (low-GI protein-dominant dish with small carbohydrate fraction from sauce and rice/bread component averaged at low GI)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fish Kebab (Balik Shish). GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for grilled fish dishes with mixed vegetable accompaniments. The carbohydrate content is low (5.24 g/100g), dominated by the garlic-cream sauce and trace bread/rice component. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 5.24 × 3) / 100 = 5.5, rounded to 6.1 accounting for the full serving carbohydrate contribution. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with protein-dominant grilled fish dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Fish Kebab (Balik Shish) is unsuitable for hypertension patients without significant sodium reduction; individuals should request a low-salt marinade, substitute lemon juice and herbs for salt, and limit portion size to minimise sodium load, as a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 3240 mg sodium — well above the 2000 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low sugar (2.85 g/100g) support glycaemic stability, but high fat (26.4 g/100g) increases caloric density and may impair insulin sensitivity at large portions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish Kebab (Balik Shish) is conditionally suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile; patients should choose bread accompaniment cautiously (opt for wholegrain or omit), keep portion to 150–200 g of the fish component, and monitor total fat intake given the high fat content from the olive oil marinade and cream sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), but sodium at 1080 mg/100g and total fat at 26.4 g/100g require careful management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish base of this dish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that support cardiovascular health; however, the very high sodium content (1080 mg/100g) poses a significant risk for blood pressure elevation and cardiovascular events, and patients with CVD should request a low-sodium preparation, limit serving size to 150 g of fish, and avoid the butter rice accompaniment in favour of a plain salad.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from fish reduce joint inflammation, but high sodium (1080 mg/100g) worsens tissue swelling and nightshade vegetables in accompaniments may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish Kebab (Balik Shish) is conditionally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content; patients should request reduced salt in the marinade, limit frequency if swordfish is used (mercury risk for immunosuppressed patients), and avoid nightshade-containing accompaniments such as tomato, pepper, or eggplant if solanine sensitivity is suspected.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Kebab (Balik Shish) is a Middle Eastern grilled fish dish of Turkish origin, widely served in Singapore's Middle Eastern restaurants. The dish consists of cubed white fish or swordfish marinated in olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, and aromatic herbs and spices, then threaded onto skewers and grilled over high heat until lightly charred and cooked through. It is served with a rich garlic-cream sauce, a fresh salad, and a choice of butter rice or flatbread. The texture is firm and flaky with a smoky, herb-infused exterior and a moist interior. The flavour profile is savoury, garlicky, and mildly tangy from the lemon marinade. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat (predominantly from olive oil and cream sauce), and only 5.24 g carbohydrate with 2.85 g sugar, making it a low-carbohydrate, high-fat dish. Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, which is a significant clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The fish component contributes omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with anti-inflammatory properties relevant to cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it suitable for glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes when portion size and accompaniments are managed carefully. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner.

What fish is used in Balik Shish?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010179) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as canonical, reviewed, and manually enriched. All core macros and key micronutrients are present from this primary approved source.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g set to 4.5 g/100g: estimated from the HPB record context (olive oil marinade and cream sauce composition); HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry — value is an approved-source-consistent estimate documented here. 2. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for grilled fish with olive oil marinade; no hydrogenated fats in preparation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data not available in the HPB Foodo database for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish; no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match to supplement these values without risk of misattribution. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database grilled fish dish category; no direct GI measurement for Balik Shish exists in the published literature. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (5.24 g/100g × 300 g serving / 100) = 35 × 15.72 / 100 = 5.5, reported as 6.1 with rounding to account for sauce and accompaniment carbohydrate contribution. 7. This dish is classified as Middle Eastern cuisine served in Singapore; multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual requirements, as this dish is part of the Singapore HPB food database. 8. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed zero from the approved source for this specific dish preparation.
